2026-06-17T15:16:14.4392504Z [36;1mset -euo pipefail[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4392905Z [36;1m# actions/download-artifact (>=v5) flattens contents directly into `path/`[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4393421Z [36;1m# whenever exactly one artifact matches the pattern, ignoring[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4393915Z [36;1m# `merge-multiple: false`. Re-shape into the per-group subdir layout so[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4394446Z [36;1m# downstream validation sees a stable structure regardless of matrix size.[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4395380Z [36;1m# Upstream reference:[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4395838Z [36;1m# https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/blob/v8.0.1/src/download-artifact.ts[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4396428Z [36;1m# (see the `isSingleArtifactDownload || mergeMultiple || artifacts.length === 1`[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4396975Z [36;1m# branch). Remove this step once download-artifact stops flattening or[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4397414Z [36;1m# exposes an opt-out.[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4397783Z [36;1mexpected_count=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq '.group // [] | length')[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4398202Z [36;1mif [ "$expected_count" -eq 1 ]; then[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4398571Z [36;1m  group_id=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq -r '.group[0].id')[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4399057Z [36;1m  # Defence-in-depth: group_id is interpolated into a shell path. apm-prep[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4399565Z [36;1m  # produces a sanitised id today, but enforce a strict allowlist here so[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4400034Z [36;1m  # any future schema drift cannot smuggle traversal sequences.[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4400505Z [36;1m  if ! printf '%s' "$group_id" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$'; then[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4400977Z [36;1m    echo "::error::unsafe group_id '$group_id' (must match ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$)"[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4401362Z [36;1m    exit 1[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4401584Z [36;1m  fi[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4401911Z [36;1m  group_dir="/tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles/${ARTIFACT_PREFIX}apm-${group_id}"[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4402320Z [36;1m  if [ ! -d "$group_dir" ]; then[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4402911Z [36;1m    mkdir -p "$group_dir"[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4403391Z [36;1m    find /tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -path "$group_dir" -exec mv {} "$group_dir/" \;[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:14.4403886Z [36;1m  fi[0m

2026-06-17T15:16:35.6779752Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:35.6780159Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:35.6780485Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-06-17T15:16:35.6780756Z [36;1m[0m

2026-06-17T15:25:08.2174261Z       "rationale": "The codebase's own ref-injection control is _validate_ref (client.py:76), which enforces ^[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9._/-]*$. It is called in _fetch_git (line 528) and _fetch_local (line 593), but NOT in _fetch_github or _fetch_gitlab. On a github-kind or gitlab-kind cache hit (fetch_marketplace returns from _read_cache without touching _fetch_file), no _validate_ref call is guaranteed before resolve_marketplace_plugin reaches the new block at line 888 and does canonical = f\"{canonical}#{source.ref}\". Downstream paths are safe -- git_cache passes the ref as a subprocess list arg (no shell=True) and GitHub API calls URL-encode the ref -- but the explicit guard the project relies on for ref injection is absent at the assembly point. A crafted marketplaces.json entry (e.g., via direct file edit bypassing apm marketplace add) could embed chars the _SAFE_REF_RE would catch but that currently flow silently into the canonical.",

2026-06-17T15:25:13.8547789Z       "rationale": "If a user runs 'apm marketplace add owner/repo --ref main' to deliberately target the main branch when the repo's actual default branch is 'master' or 'develop', the guard at lines 885-886 treats 'main' as a no-op and drops the pin. The canonical carries no #ref, so the downloader resolves the repo's real default branch. The PR comment (line 876) acknowledges this but frames it as a known limitation. From a trust standpoint this means the registry's explicitly-stated pinning contract is silently ignored: two machines with different repo default branches will install different commits from the same lockfile entry.",
2026-06-17T15:25:15.0160325Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/marketplace/resolver.py",

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9340171Z [88] ◐ Let me analyze this PR carefully from an auth expert perspective.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9340518Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9341003Z   The PR is about fixing marketplace `--ref` not being propagated to relative plugin sources. Let me look at the diff more carefully:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9341522Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9342052Z   1. The first change is in how `effective_ref` is calculated for GitLab in-marketplace dependency references
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9342784Z   2. The second change appends `#{source.ref}` to a canonical URL
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9343215Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9344109Z   The key question for auth activation: Does this PR change authentication behavior, token management, credential resolution, host classification used by AuthResolver, git or HTTP authorization headers, or remote-host fallback semantics?
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9345312Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9345576Z   Let me think about this:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9345961Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9365326Z   1. The canonical URL is later used by the downloader
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9366878Z   2. The `effective_ref` change is specifically for GitLab (`_gitlab_in_marketplace_dependency_reference`), while the canonical URL change appends `#{source.ref}` as a URL fragment to specify branches/tags/commits that are parsed later to determine what to fetch. I'm trying to understand how these canonical URLs work in the auth context.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9368045Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9370181Z   For GitHub and GitLab, the canonical URLs look like `https://github.com/owner/repo` or `https://github.com/owner/repo#ref`, where the fragment determines the git ref to checkout. The key insight is that the hostname stays the same—only the fragment changes—so the AuthResolver, which classifies hosts as github.com vs gitlab.com, shouldn't be affected by these ref changes. The `_gitlab_in_marketplace_dependency_reference` function is GitLab-specific and doesn't alter the host either, so the auth path should remain unchanged.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9372064Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9373945Z   Looking at the actual PR diff to understand the full scope of how refs are being propagated... Continuing to examine the code changes, the second modification appends the source ref as a fragment to the canonical URL when certain conditions are met—specifically when there's no dep_ref, no version spec, the source is a string, there's no existing fragment, and the ref isn't a default branch like main or HEAD. This ensures the ref informati... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2556 chars]

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9409340Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9409844Z      - `src/apm_cli/marketplace/resolver.py` (+30/-1 lines)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9410335Z      - `tests/unit/marketplace/test_marketplace_resolver.py` (+101 lines)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9410700Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9410934Z   2. **What the changes do**:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9411950Z      - In the GitLab path: Adds an `effective_ref` computation before calling `_gitlab_in_marketplace_dependency_reference`. This involves string comparisons (`source.ref not in ("main", "HEAD")`), a boolean guard (`if not effective_ref and source.ref and ...`), and a ternary-style assignment.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9414106Z      - In the GitHub path: Adds a conditional block that appends the source ref to the canonical string when several conditions are met—the dependency ref is unset, there's no version spec, the plugin source is a string, there's no existing fragment, and the ref isn't a default branch. This is a string comparison plus conditional concatenation.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9415522Z      - The test file adds 101 lines of unit tests covering these scenarios.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9415892Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9416103Z   3. **Performance surface assessment**:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9416551Z      - None of the changed files are in the fast-path set: `src/apm_cli/cache/**`, `src/apm_cli/deps`
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9416969Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9418941Z   The changes are purely pre-download metadata computation—string comparisons, boolean guards, and f-string concatenation with no network calls, cache lookups, filesystem operations, or parallelism changes. Since the modifications affect the `resolve_marketplace_plugin` function which runs before any download happens, the wall-time impact is negligible. The PR only touches `src/apm_cli/marketplace/resolver.py` and its test file, neither in the performance fast-path, so the conclusion is `active: false`.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9420673Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9437448Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9437713Z   From a growth hacker perspective, here are my observations:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9439950Z   This fix addresses a real pain point for teams using marketplace-based installs with custom refs—when they specify `--ref feat/xxx` for testing pre-release versions, they expect all downstream installs to respect that, so the silent fallback to the default branch is genuinely frustrating for enterprise setups. The contributor sergio-sisternes-epam is already active in the community (they've contributed to Antigravity CLI), and this is a textbook example of good community contribution: clear bug report, focused fix, solid test coverage.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9441740Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9443739Z   However, there's a gap worth flagging—the CHANGELOG doesn't have an entry for this fix yet. Since it's a user-facing bug that resolves silent incorrect behavior, it deserves a CHANGELOG entry so affected users know to upgrade. The story here is compelling too: "APM now correctly honors --ref across all plugin source types" matters to marketplace authors, especially teams like EPAM testing custom refs. And sergio-sisternes-epam becoming a repeat contributor is worth recognizing—that's the kind of engagement worth highlighting.

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9484263Z [152] ◐ Now I have the full diff. Let me analyze it from an auth perspective.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9484623Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9484824Z   Key observations:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9485175Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9486332Z   1. **The canonical URL change** (lines 880-888): Appends `#{source.ref}` to the canonical URL. The canonical URL format in APM appears to be `owner/repo/path` (without a host prefix for GitHub.com), e.g., `acme/catalog/plugins/my-plugin`. Adding `#feat/my-feature` gives `acme/catalog/plugins/my-plugin#feat/my-feature`. The host is **not** changed by this operation.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9487530Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9488059Z   2. **The GitLab dep_ref change** (lines 828-833): For GitLab sources, the `effective_ref` is propagated to `_gitlab_in_marketplace_dependency_reference`. 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9488638Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9490568Z   3. **The DependencyReference parsing** (lines 836-855): When creating a `DependencyReference` object with the ref set, the host field is what drives auth routing, not the ref itself. The comment at line 836 explains the existing logic for GitHub Enterprise hosts—the bare canonical drops the host information that `DependencyReference.parse` needs to route auth correctly to enterprise hosts instead of defaulting to `github.com`. The new block (lines 880-888) only executes when `dep_ref` is present.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9492214Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9493897Z   Actually, I need to reconsider the control flow here. The host-backfill block (843-854) runs when `dep_ref is None AND _is_in_marketplace_source AND _needs_canonical_host_prefix`, modifying canonical to include the source host. The ref-propagation block (880-888) has different conditions. Both could potentially execute in sequence—the host backfill fires first and updates canonical, then the ref logic would follow.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9495370Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9496527Z   So after host backfill, canonical becomes something like `ghe.example.com/acme/catalog/plugins/my-plugin`, and then the ref-propagation block appends the fragment, resulting in `ghe.example.com/acme/catalog/plugins/my-plugin#feat/xxx`. The host prefix stays intact for auth routing while the ref gets added as a fragment, which is the correct behavior.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9497811Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9498356Z   For the test cases, I'm checking that GitHub relative sources get the ref appended without a host prefix (st... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2733 chars]

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9507019Z [162] ◐ Now I have a good picture. Let me summarize my findings:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9507352Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9508312Z   1. **CHANGELOG**: There is NO entry for issue #1811 in the `## [Unreleased]` section. The unreleased section has entries for other fixes but not this one. The PR fixes a real user-visible bug where `apm install plugin@marketplace` didn't use the registered `--ref` for relative string plugin sources.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9509331Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9509549Z   2. **Documentation on `--ref` behavior**: 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9510272Z      - `docs/src/content/docs/reference/cli/marketplace.md` mentions `--ref` with the description "Git ref (branch, tag, or SHA). Default: `main`. Applies only to git-backed sources. 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9511085Z      - For HTTPS git URLs, a `#ref` fragment is equivalent and is stored as the ref."
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9512057Z      - `docs/src/content/docs/consumer/installing-from-marketplaces.md` mentions: "Git HTTPS URLs with `#ref` -- APM strips the fragment into the stored ref. If you omit `#ref` (or `--ref`), APM warns because the default branch can move."
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9513061Z      - Neither document mentions that `--ref` is propagated to **relative** plugin sources.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9513459Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9514067Z   3. **Gap analysis**: The `--ref` flag description only says it applies to "git-backed sources" but doesn't clarify what happens when a relative plugin source is used with the flag.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9514726Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9516707Z   Before this fix, the ref was silently dropped for relative sources, but now it's propagated correctly—a behavioral change that should be documented. The CHANGELOG is missing an entry for #1811, and the CLI reference could be clearer about how `--ref` now works with relative plugin sources, including the caveats around `main`/`HEAD`. I should recommend adding a CHANGELOG entry in the `## [Unreleased]` > `### Fixed` section and updating the `--ref` flag description in the docs.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9518243Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9532862Z 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9533314Z [165] ✓ grep(-n: true, output_mode: content, paths: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/ma..., pattern: def fetch_or_cache, ...)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9533756Z 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9533982Z [166] ◐ Now I have a complete picture. Let me make my assessment:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9534318Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9535120Z   1. **Host-backfill block (843-854)**: Runs before the new ref-propagation block (869-888). It adds the host prefix to canonical for enterprise hosts. This is the auth routing mechanism, and it's unaffected by the new code.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9535909Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9536838Z   2. **Ref-propagation block (869-888)**: New code. Fires AFTER the host-backfill. Adds `#ref` fragment to canonical. The `#` fragment doesn't affect host classification. Even if both blocks fire (enterprise host + ref propagation), the sequence is:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9537841Z      - Host backfill produces `ghe.example.com/acme/catalog/plugins/my-plugin`
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9538439Z      - Ref propagation appends the fragment to get `ghe.example.com/acme/catalog/plugins/my-plugin#feat/xxx`
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9539048Z      - Auth routing still reads `ghe.example.com` and routes correctly to enterprise auth
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9539568Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9540397Z   3. **GitLab change (828-833)**: The `effective_ref` modification only touches the `reference` field in the resulting `DependencyReference`, leaving the `host` field untouched. Since auth routing depends on the host, this change is completely neutral.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9541279Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9541865Z   This PR is definitively auth-neutral—the changes only affect ref handling, not the host-based routing that determines authentication.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9542338Z 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9542979Z [167] ◆ The picture is clear. Let me verify one more thing — whether `DependencyReference.parse` ever dispatches auth based on the fragment/ref rather than purely the host component:

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9556554Z   4. **The known silent-drop edge case**: There's a documented comment acknowledging that if `source.ref == "main"` but the repo's actual default branch is named differently (e.g., `master`), the ref gets silently dropped with no logging or warning. This is the exact behavioral edge case the original bug (#1811) was about, yet it remains unlogged.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9557681Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9559149Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9580331Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9580715Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9582213Z   angle: This fix addresses a real pain point for enterprise teams using `--ref` to test feature branches across marketplaces—they'd silently get incorrect behavior without knowing to upgrade. 2026-06-17T15:41:03.9606450Z [183] ◐ This confirms it conclusively. `resolve_for_dep` uses `dep_ref.host`, `dep_ref.port`, and `dep_ref.host_type` for auth resolution. The `reference` field (which is what's being changed in this PR for GitLab) is not involved in auth routing at all.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9607315Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9608157Z   For the canonical-based path (when `dep_ref is None`), the canonical is parsed by `DependencyReference.parse` downstream, which extracts the host from the URL's prefix - not from the `#fragment` part.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9608904Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9609197Z   This PR is definitively auth-neutral. I'll return `active: false`.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9609462Z 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9611425Z [184] ◆ The analysis is complete. `resolve_for_dep` dispatches auth exclusively on `dep_ref.host`, `dep_ref.port`, and `dep_ref.host_type`. The `reference` field (what both hunks modify) is never consulted for auth routing. For the canonical-path (when `dep_ref is None`), `DependencyReference.parse` reads the host prefix — the `#fragment` portion is discarded before host extraction. The existing host-backfill block (lines 843–854, auth routing for `*.ghe.com`, issue #1285) runs *before* the new ref-propagation block and is not disturbed by it.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9613244Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9613831Z     "persona": "auth-expert",

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9618760Z [185] ◐ Now I need to understand the important code path where `_validate_ref` might NOT be called. Looking at `fetch_marketplace`:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9619289Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9619923Z   1. If `use_sidecar_cache and not force_refresh` (line 913): The cache is hit on line 914, and `_validate_ref` is NOT called! The function returns from cached data without validating the ref.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9620594Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9620873Z   2. For `url` kind: `_validate_ref` is NOT called for URL marketplaces.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9621219Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9621639Z   3. For `_fetch_file` calls (line 967): This calls `_fetch_local` or `_fetch_git` internally, which do call `_validate_ref`.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9623641Z   This reveals a potential security issue: when the sidecar cache is hit, `_validate_ref` gets skipped entirely, so if a malicious ref value somehow made it into the cached `MarketplaceSource` object, it would pass through unvalidated. However, since the `MarketplaceSource` comes from the local `~/.apm/marketplaces.json` configuration file, which is user-controlled (or admin-controlled in enterprise setups), the risk depends on whether that file can be compromised. Let me trace through `_fetch_file` to see how it handles this.

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9651145Z [193] ◐ I see that the `effective_ref` comes from user input (`--ref` option) and is **not validated** before being stored in `marketplaces.json`. The ref is only validated lazily, at `_validate_ref` call time inside `_fetch_git` and `_fetch_local`, not during registration.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9652065Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9652384Z   Now let me understand the complete trust chain for the new code in PR #1824:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9652756Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9653452Z   1. User runs `apm marketplace add owner/repo --ref feat/xxx` → `effective_ref = "feat/xxx"` → stored as `source.ref` in `~/.apm/marketplaces.json` **without validation**
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9655189Z   2. When installing a plugin from that marketplace, the stored ref is retrieved from disk and used in the fetch operation. If the cache hits for certain marketplace types, `_validate_ref` is skipped entirely. Only on cache misses does the validation happen during the actual fetch. The new code then constructs a canonical URL by appending the ref to the base URL.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9656407Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9658048Z   The key question is whether that ref gets validated before being used. On cache hits, the answer is no—the ref flows directly into the canonical string without validation. But there's a subtlety: if the cache was populated during the initial `add` operation, the ref would have been validated then through the fetch process. However, I need to trace through exactly which code path handles the marketplace fetch to be certain.

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9660780Z [195] ◐ Now I understand the code flow clearly. Let me trace through what happens in the GitLab test:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9661218Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9661459Z   1. `plugin.source = "registry/my-plugin"` (a string)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9661984Z   2. `_source_needs_explicit_git_path(source)` returns True (because host is `gitlab.com` and kind is `gitlab`)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9662650Z   3. `_is_in_marketplace_source(plugin, source)` returns True (because string sources always return True)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9663199Z   4. `_extract_in_repo_path_and_ref(plugin, plugin_root="")` is called
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9663594Z      - `src = "registry/my-plugin"` (a string)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9663960Z      - After stripping leading `./`, `rel = "registry/my-plugin"`
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9664432Z      - Since the path contains a slash, the `plugin_root` prefix logic is skipped
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9664999Z      - Returns `("registry/my-plugin", None)` for the in-repo path and ref
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9665895Z   5. With `in_repo_path` set to a truthy value, the effective ref becomes `None` (since both `version_spec` and `path_ref` are `None`), and the condition checking whether an explicit ref is needed continues evaluating.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9667158Z   6. When `ref="feat/my-feature"` is provided, the condition `not effective_ref and source.ref and source.ref not in ("main", "HEAD")` evaluates to true, so `effective_ref` gets set to `"feat/my-feature"`.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9668726Z   7. This triggers the call to generate the marketplace dependency reference with the feature branch, which is what the GitLab tests are validating—the first new code block handling explicit git paths, not the second block that deals with unresolved references.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9669674Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9671761Z   Now I need to examine several gaps in the test coverage: the `"#" not in canonical` guard that prevents duplicate refs, whether HEAD is properly excluded alongside main, how string sources with resolved refs are handled, and whether there's a test for when canonical already contains a `#` from a prior resolution. So the new code path only triggers when both `version_spec` and `path_ref` are None, meaning dict sources with a ref field won't hit this branch. For the GitHub path, I'm noticing there's no test coverage for HEAD exclusion even though the code checks for it, and I should also consider when the canonical URL ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2379 chars]

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9679982Z 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9681507Z [203] ◐ Now I have a much clearer picture. The `_fetch_github` function doesn't call `_validate_ref`. However, the `ref` is passed to the GitHub REST API as a query parameter (`?ref=<ref>`), not as a shell argument. So for GitHub kind sources (the most common case), the ref is already URL-encoded via `quote(source.ref, safe="")` in `_github_raw_contents_url` (I saw this in the search results earlier - `encoded_ref = quote(source.ref, safe="")`).
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9682912Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9683979Z   So for GitHub/GitLab API paths, the ref is URL-encoded before being used in HTTP requests, which protects against most injection attacks on the network side. The `_validate_ref` pattern is `^[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9._/\-]*$` which is enforced for `_fetch_git` and `_fetch_local` subprocess paths where injection is higher risk.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9686238Z   Now looking at the new code in PR #1824, the `source.ref` gets appended directly to the canonical string, which is then parsed by `DependencyReference.parse()` to extract the reference part and pass it to git fetching commands. The key concern is whether there's a code path where a malformed `source.ref` could reach the canonical string without going through `_validate_ref` first, particularly for GitHub sources.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9687607Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9688323Z   I'm tracing through the flow to see if `DependencyReference.parse()` or downstream code validates the reference field before it gets passed to git operations, or if the validation happens somewhere else in the chain.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9689032Z 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9689322Z [204] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/ca..., view_range: [1, 80])

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9690131Z [205] ✓ grep(output_mode: content, paths: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/ca..., pattern: subprocess|run|shell|Popen|ref, type: py, ...)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9690613Z 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9691042Z [206] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me analyze the change through my DevX UX Expert lens and generate the findings.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9691634Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9691829Z   **Analysis:**
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9692027Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9692337Z   1. **User-promise surface: Does `--ref X` mean "use ref X for plugins"?**
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9692695Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9694192Z   The documentation says `--ref` "applies only to git-backed sources" for the marketplace. The user's expectation is clear: if I pin a marketplace to `--ref feat/xxx`, I want plugins from that marketplace to use `feat/xxx`. The PR fixes this gap — before this fix, `apm install plugin@marketplace` would download from the default branch, breaking the user's mental model. This is the correct fix.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9695667Z   2. **The "main"/"HEAD" exclusion: Is this surprising?**
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9695981Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9697288Z   There's a notable UX friction here. The docs state the default is `main`, so when users don't specify `--ref`, the stored `source.ref` becomes `"main"`. But the fix explicitly excludes `main` and `HEAD` from being applied, meaning if someone explicitly passes `--ref main`, it gets silently ignored rather than honored. This creates a confusing gap between what the documentation promises and what actually happens.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9698626Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9700220Z   The real problem emerges in edge cases: if a repo's actual default branch isn't `main` but something like `trunk`, and a user intentionally pins `--ref main` to lock onto that specific branch, the flag would be silently dropped. The PR author recognizes this limitation—acknowledging that `main` and `HEAD` are excluded, but if the repo's actual default branch differs from these names, the behavior becomes unpredictable.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9701601Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9703134Z   That said, this isn't technically a regression from the PR itself. Before this change, `--ref main` was already being ignored, so the behavior for that specific case remains unchanged. The PR actually improves handling for other refs. The underlying UX concern—whether users should be warned that `--ref main` is treated as "default branch"—is a separate issue entirely and not something this PR introduces.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9704481Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9705024Z   Now looking at idempotency and error handling: running the same install command twice w... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 7930 chars]
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9705496Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9711825Z   1. The `--ref` docs say "Default: `main`" - so when a user doesn't specify `--ref`, source.ref will be "main". The exclusion of "main" is necessary to avoid injecting `#main` in the common case where the user didn't specify a ref.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9712598Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9713905Z

The idempotency is preserved correctly by this fix.

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9738980Z [215] ◐ Now I see that git commands use `subprocess.run` with lists, not shell=True. So the ref is passed as a separate argument to subprocess (line 245: `cmd.append(ref)`) and not subject to shell injection. This is the correct security posture.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9739816Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9740011Z   Looking at the subprocess calls:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9740391Z   - `subprocess.run(cmd, ...)` with `cmd` as a list and `shell=False` (default)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9740879Z   - The ref is appended to the cmd list, not concatenated into a shell string
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9741248Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9741608Z   So the concern about shell injection is mitigated by the use of subprocess with argument lists.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9742028Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9742895Z   However, there's still a secondary concern: what does `_validate_ref` actually check? It validates: `^[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9._/\-]*$`. This is called in `_fetch_git` and `_fetch_local` paths, but **not** in `_fetch_github` path (which calls the GitHub API with URL-encoded refs).
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9745081Z   The new PR code at lines 880-888 uses `source.ref` directly in the canonical string without explicit validation at that point, though the ref ultimately gets passed safely downstream either as a subprocess argument or URL-encoded. The real question is whether `source.ref` could contain problematic characters like newlines or null bytes that might cause issues, and whether the `_SAFE_REF_RE` pattern adequately covers all edge cases.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9746511Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9747484Z   The "main"/"HEAD" exclusion guard is a correctness optimization rather than a security measure, but there's a subtle issue: if the repository's actual default branch has a different name (like "master" or "develop"), hardcoding "main" as the exclusion could miss cases where the ref should be suppressed.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9748502Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9749353Z   The `source.ref` comes from the user's local config file, which is a trusted boundary. The `"#" not in canonical` check prevents double-hashes as intended, though there's a potential edge case if the canonical string already contains a hash from another source.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9750252Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9751189Z   The new code ensures the ref gets captured in the lockfile for better determinism, but there's a question about whether it's recording the mutable branch name or the actual commit SHA—if it'... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 5321 chars]

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9787360Z   **Coverage gaps:**
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9787580Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9789532Z   The HEAD ref exclusion condition isn't being tested in either code path, even though both blocks explicitly filter it out alongside "main".  This creates a regression risk where removing HEAD from the exclusion tuple could slip through undetected. Additionally, while the unit tests validate the resolver logic correctly, there's no integration-level coverage proving that the install pipeline actually uses this corrected canonical when running `apm install plugin@marketplace --ref`. The unit tests are solid regression traps for the resolver itself, but the end-to-end install flow surface isn't verified.

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9801566Z 
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9801789Z [235] ◐ Now I have the full picture. Let me summarize what I found:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9802116Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9802998Z   The `plugin.source` string is validated through `_normalise_relative_plugin_source` → `validate_path_segments(rel, ...)` which guards against path traversal. This is done before the new PR's code runs.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9803741Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9804026Z   So the flow for a GitHub-family marketplace with a string source is:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9804913Z   1. `resolve_plugin_source` → `_resolve_relative_source` → `_normalise_relative_plugin_source` → `validate_path_segments` (path traversal check on `plugin.source`)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9805639Z   2. Returns canonical like `owner/repo/plugins/my-plugin` (no `#`)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9806411Z   3. The new code at lines 880-888 checks if `"#"` is absent from the canonical string, then appends `#source.ref` to it, resulting in something like `owner/repo/plugins/my-plugin#source.ref`
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9807082Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9807759Z   So `plugin.source` gets validated by `validate_path_segments`, but `source.ref` is appended without an explicit validation call at that injection point, though it may be validated on other code paths.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9808499Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9808848Z   **Finding 1: source.ref lacks explicit validation at the assembly point (recommended)**
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9809256Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9811810Z   While `source.ref` passes through `_validate_ref` on git and local fetch paths, it skips that validation on GitHub and GitLab API paths. The downstream protections—subprocess argument lists without shell execution and URL encoding—do prevent exploitation in known scenarios, but the explicit guard at the canonical assembly point is missing. Adding `_validate_ref(source.ref, source_name=marketplace_name)` before line 888 would formalize this guarantee as defense-in-depth. The real question is whether this is truly blocking or just recommended, given that the git subprocess calls use list arguments rather than shell execution, and the GitHub API uses URL-encoded refs.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9814010Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9814841Z   Since a user who can write to `~/.apm/marketplaces.json` can already do much worse things, and the attack surface is quite narrow, I'm rating this as recommended rather than blocking, with the note that `_validate_ref` should be called at the assembly point.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9815797Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9816075Z   **Finding 2: The "mai... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4278 chars]
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9816348Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9822067Z [241] ◐ Good. The lockfile captures both `resolved_commit` (SHA) and `resolved_ref` (mutable ref name). The `resolved_commit` field is the SHA that pins the install deterministically. With this PR, the canonical now carries the `#ref` suffix, which means the lockfile will record the `resolved_ref` correctly and the SHA will be resolved from it. This is correct behavior for determinism.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9823410Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9823652Z   Now let me compile my final security review findings:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9823964Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9824156Z   **Summary:**
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9824355Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9826429Z   1. **Recommended**: `source.ref` is appended to the canonical at line 888 without an inline `_validate_ref` call. The only code paths where `_validate_ref` is guaranteed to have run are `_fetch_git` and `_fetch_local` - the GitHub API and GitLab API paths (the most common marketplace sources) do not call `_validate_ref`. On a cache hit for GitHub-kind sources, no ref validation has occurred before line 888. While downstream git subprocess calls use argument lists (no shell injection), and GitHub API uses URL-encoded refs, the explicit guard that the codebase uses for ref injection protection is absent at the assembly point.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9828427Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9829511Z   2. **Nit**: The `"main"` / `"HEAD"` exclusion silently swallows an explicitly user-pinned ref. A user who runs `apm marketplace add owner/repo --ref main` to explicitly target the `main` branch (when the repo's default is `master`) will have their pin silently dropped. This creates a trust gap where `source.ref` isn't always honored as specified.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9830647Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9831279Z   3. **Nit**: The explanatory comment justifying the `"main"/"HEAD"` exclusion appears in the GitHub-family block but not consistently elsewhere, leaving the rationale partially documented.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9831975Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9832170Z   Now I'm returning the JSON.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9832342Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9847296Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9847543Z   Looking at the code:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9847878Z   - Block 2 is only entered when `isinstance(plugin.source, str)` is True
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9848484Z   - For string sources, `resolve_plugin_source` calls `_resolve_relative_source` which returns `owner/repo/path` (no `#`)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9849359Z   - BUT: block 2 comes AFTER the host-backfill block (which only prepends `host/`), and after `_compute_cross_repo_misconfig_risk` (which doesn't modify canonical)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9850115Z   - So for string sources, canonical can never have `#` before block 2 is reached
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9850488Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9851220Z   The guard appears to be defensive code that wouldn't trigger in the current implementation's string-source path, though it could become relevant if the source resolution logic changes or takes a non-standard route.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9851997Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9852976Z   The `isinstance(plugin.source, str)` check keeps us on the string path where canonical never contains `#`, making that condition always true. 2026-06-17T15:41:03.9863460Z [247] ◐ Now I have a good understanding of the code. Let me analyze the PR carefully:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9863847Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9864041Z   ## Architecture Analysis
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9864280Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9864479Z   ### What's happening in this PR
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9864742Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9865433Z   The PR fixes a bug where a marketplace registered with `--ref feat/xxx` doesn't propagate that ref to relative string plugin sources when resolving `apm install plugin@marketplace`.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9866201Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9866430Z   ### Structure of `resolve_marketplace_plugin`
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9866716Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9867025Z   The function has several sequential "phases" as it builds up the canonical:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9867394Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9867845Z   1. **Local marketplace fast-path** (line 797-808): If source_kind == "local" and isinstance(plugin.source, str), early return
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9868459Z   2. **Compute initial canonical** via `resolve_plugin_source` (line 810-815)
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9869228Z   3. **GitLab/non-GitHub explicit-git-path block** (line 818-834): For non-GitHub hosts, construct a `DependencyReference` with virtual path and ref
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9869944Z   4. **GHE host backfill** (line 836-854): For `*.ghe.com` hosts, inject the host prefix
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9870507Z   5. **Cross-repo misconfig sentinel** (line 856-867): Build the `CrossRepoMisconfigRisk` detection
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9871393Z   6. **[NEW] Ref propagation** (line 869-888): The key fix—for relative string sources on GitHub family hosts, append `#source.ref` if not already present
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9872183Z   7. **Version spec override** (line 890+): When `version_spec` is explicitly set, it takes precedence over the ref
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9872645Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9873646Z   The new ref propagation block checks that we're on the GitHub path, no version override exists, the source is a relative string, there's no existing ref anchor, and the ref isn't a default branch like main or HEAD—then it appends the ref to the canonical path.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9874545Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9875221Z   The GitLab path already handles this correctly by using `version_spec` as the highest priority, falling back to `path_ref` and then `source.ref` when neither of the first two are set.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9875892Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9876155Z   Now I'm identifying the actual issues with this approach.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9876472Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9877618Z   The `"HEAD"` exclusion is problematic because HEAD is a symbolic reference that would never actually appear in `source.ref` as a user-specified value—it's what gets checked out during cloning, not what gets stored. The real gap is that the code on... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 19400 chars]
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9878556Z

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9882487Z [252] ◐ Now I have a complete understanding of the code. Let me also check whether there are any dict in-marketplace sources without explicit ref that would also be affected on GHE hosts. Looking at the GitHub ref propagation block:
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9883341Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9883525Z   ```python
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9883727Z   if (
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9883916Z       dep_ref is None
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9884149Z       and not version_spec

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9885946Z       canonical = f"{canonical}#{source.ref}"

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9886575Z   
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9887414Z   The `isinstance(plugin.source, str)` guard means dict sources (github type) without explicit refs are NOT fixed by this PR on GHE hosts. But for dict sources, the ref is typically already in the dict itself, so this is less likely to be a problem in practice.
2026-06-17T15:41:03.9889730Z   Let me trace through what happens with a GHE host and a relative string source path: the GHE backfill produces a full canonical URL, then the ref propagation block checks all its conditions—the dependency has no explicit ref, no version spec, the source is a string, there's no hash in the canonical yet, and the source has a non-default ref like `feat/xxx`—so the ref should get appended to the canonical.

2026-06-17T15:41:03.9914062Z         "rationale": "Both new blocks gate on `source.ref not in (\"main\", \"HEAD\")`, but the new test class only covers ref=\"main\" (tests 2 and 5). to specify what pull request data needs to be retrieved from GitHub. \nPossible options: \n 1. get - Get details of a specific pull request.\n 2. get_diff - Get the diff of a pull request.\n 3. get_status - Get combined commit status of a head commit in a pull request.\n 4. get_files - Get the list of files changed in a pull request. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.\n 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. Returns threads with metadata (isResolved, isOutdated, isCollapsed) and their associated comments. Use cursor-based pagination (perPage, after) to control results.\n 6. get_reviews - Get the reviews on a pull request. When asked for review comments, use get_review_comments method.\n 7. get_comments - Get comments on a pull request. Use this if user doesn't specifically want review comments. 2026-06-17T15:41:04.2348508Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-17T15:17:15.149Z","event":"rpc_response","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"IN","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":1824,\"title\":\"Fix marketplace --ref not propagated to relative plugin sources (#1811)\",\"body\":\"## TL;DR\\n\\nPropagate the marketplace\\u0026#39;s registered `--ref` to relative string plugin sources so `apm install plugin@marketplace` resolves against the registered branch, not the default branch.\\n\\n## Problem (WHY)\\n\\nWhen a marketplace is registered with `apm marketplace add owner/repo --ref feat/xxx`, and its `marketplace.json` uses relative string sources (e.g. `\\u0026#34;./plugins/my-plugin\\u0026#34;`), `apm install my-plugin@my-marketplace` downloads the plugin from the **default branch** instead of the registered `--ref`.\\n\\nThe ref is correctly persisted and used to fetch `marketplace.json`, but `resolve_marketplace_plugin` calls `resolve_plugin_source` without passing `source.ref`. The canonical string has no `#ref` suffix, so the downloader resolves against the default branch. The same gap exists in the GitLab structured-ref path where `_gitlab_in_marketplace_dependency_reference` receives `None` for string sources.\\n\\n## Approach (WHAT)\\n\\nTwo surgical changes in `resolve_marketplace_plugin`:\\n\\n1. **GitHub-family path:** After computing the canonical, append `#source.ref` when the plugin is a relative string source, no `#ref` is already present, no explicit `version_spec` overrides it, and `source.ref` is not `\\u0026#34;main\\u0026#34;` or `\\u0026#34;HEAD\\u0026#34;`.\\n\\n2. **GitLab/non-GitHub structured path:** Fall back to `source.ref` in the ref argument to `_gitlab_in_marketplace_dependency_reference` when `path_ref` is `None` and no `version_spec`.\\n\\n## Validation\\n\\n- 5 new regression tests covering both GitHub and GitLab hosts (ref propagation, main exclusion, version_spec override)\\n- All 111 existing + new tests pass\\n- Lint (ruff check, ruff format, pylint R0801) clean\\n\\nFixes #1811\",\"state\":\"open\",\"draft\":false,\"merged\":false,\"mergeable_state\":\"blocked\",\"html_url\":\"https://github.com/microsoft/apm/pull/1824\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"sergio-sisternes-epam\",\"id\":207026618,\"profile_url\":\"https://github.com/sergio-sisternes-epam\",\"avatar_url\":\"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/207026618?v=4\"},\"labels\":[\"panel-review\"],\"requested_reviewers\":[\"danielmeppiel\"],\"head\":{\"ref\":\"sergio-sisternes-epam/fix-marketplace-ref-propagation\",\"sha\":\"[REDACTED]\",\"repo\":{\"full_name\":\"microsoft/apm\",\"description\":\"Agent Package Manager\"}},\"base\":{\"ref\":\"main\",\"sha\":\"[REDACTED]\",\"repo\":{\"full_name\":\"microsoft/apm\",\"description\":\"Agent Package Manager\"}},\"additions\":131,\"deletions\":1,\"changed_files\":2,\"commits\":1,\"created_at\":\"2026-06-17T11:45:21Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-06-17T15:13:07Z\"}","type":"text"}]}}}
